visits.text: Lake Achensee, Tyrol's largest lake (9 km long, 1.5 km wide) at 930 meters altitude between the Karwendel and Rofan massifs. The exceptionally clear water (visibility up to 10 meters) and its turquoise hues shifting with the light make it one of the most beautiful lakes in the Austrian Alps.

Village of Pertisau on the lake's west shore, preserved with its traditional alpine architecture. Lake center and starting point for all activities: boating, hikes toward the Karwendel, via ferratas. The 18th-century Baroque church is worth a look.

Boat cruise on the Achensee with Achenseeschifffahrt company (130 years of history, 6 piers). The MS Stella Maris (1912) was Austria's first diesel-powered boat. Complete crossing Pertisau-Achenkirch in 40 minutes, possible stop at Gaisalm accessible only by boat or on foot.

activities.text: Gaisalm hike from Pertisau (8.8 km, 2h) along the wild east shore with no vehicle road. Isolated Gaisalm refuge accessible only by this trail or by boat. Return by boat possible. Intermediate level, well-marked trail.

Achensee Steam Railway (Achenseebahn, 1889), narrow-gauge track covering 6.79 km linking Jenbach to the Achensee with 440 meters of elevation gain. The original steam locomotive is Austria's oldest still in service. Departures from Jenbach (15 min by car from Pertisau) from April to October.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 days in Austria: Wachau Valley, Alpine Peaks & Salzburg

visits.text: No monastic library in the world approaches the hall at Stift Admont: 70 metres long, 14 wide, 11 high, 70,000 old books and frescoes by Bartolomeo Altomonte painted when he was over 80 (1775-1776). The sculptures by Josef Stammel - 'The Four Last Things' - are disturbingly modern. The monastery, founded in 1074, is the oldest in Styria. Reopened after restoration in 2008. Allow 1h30.

Behind the Stift, the wild Enns rushes through the limestone gorges of the Gesäuse in permanent thunder. The suspension bridge at Haindlkar offers the best plunge into this landscape. The walls of Hochtor (2,369 m) drop almost vertically into the river.

Gesäuse National Park, created in 2002, covers 120 km² of gorges and primary forest. It's Styria's only national park. The visitor centre in Admont presents the geology and wildlife - lynx and golden eagle are present, discreet.

activities.text: If you only do one hike in the Gesäuse, choose the Haindlkar from the Enns car park - 2 hours return, high glacial valley landscape with the Haller Mauern range as backdrop. Marked trail, intermediate level.
Lunch at the Stiftskeller before leaving - the monastic tavern's kitchen serves regional specialities and the Stift's own wine. Terrace on the Stiftsgarten facing the pond.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Styria - Ausseerland, Gesäuse and wine country

visits.text: The best WWII story you've never heard - and it takes place under the Altaussee mountain. The Nazis had hidden Michelangelo's Bruges Madonna, the Ghent Altarpiece by the Van Eyck brothers and hundreds of other works here. The miners sabotaged the explosives to save them. This story, immortalised in the film The Monuments Men, can be visited at Salzwelten Altaussee, Austria's largest active salt mine - operating since the 12th century. The special guided tour 'Bomben auf Michelangelo' takes place every Friday at 4pm, booking required.

Lake Altaussee gleams deep blue beneath the Totes Gebirge. The Seenrundweg circles it on 7 km of flat path - ideal for digesting the mine visit. The fishermen's houses and their colourful boats remind you that this lake is still living, not museumified.

activities.text: Complete the Altausseer Seenrundweg in 2 hours at your own pace - the path follows both shores, the north bank in full sun in the afternoon, the south bank in shade. Stop at the Fischerhütte for grilled lake trout.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Styria - Ausseerland, Gesäuse and wine country

visits.text: Two monasteries for the price of one: Stift Altenburg, founded in 1144, conceals beneath its sumptuous baroque building the entirety of a medieval convent, rediscovered during excavations in 1994. Scriptoria, chapter house and 12th-century Romanesque cloister lie intact beneath the parquet floors of the gilded halls. Entrance 12 euros, under-18s free, open May-October.

The library of Stift Altenburg measures 50 metres long and its Paul Troger frescoes cover the entire vault: the largest concentration of this Tyrolean painter's works in a single building. The Arnold Collection wing, added in 2018, brings together nearly 300 private baroque paintings donated to the abbey by a Tyrolean collector.

activities.text: If you arrive on Saturday or Sunday, a guided tour departs at 11am and 2pm from the foyer - no reservation needed, included in the entrance ticket. It covers the baroque, the medieval convent and the themed gardens. On other weekdays, the self-guided tour with included map works perfectly well. Abbey restaurant open for lunch in season.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: Locals call it the 'capital of Trachten' - the dirndl and Tyrolean costume are still worn here daily, not for tourists. Bad Aussee is also the town of the famous Fasching carnival, first documented in 1768. The Marktplatz with its fountains and 19th-century bourgeois houses deserves an hour.

The Kammerhofmuseum, housed in a late-Gothic building first mentioned in 1395, was the former administrative seat of the salt trade until 1926. Across 800 m² of exhibition space, it covers salt history, folk traditions and the Aussee carnival. The Emperors' Hall with its frescoes from 1740-65 is the highlight of the visit.

The Kurpromenade follows the Ausseer Traun for several hundred metres among willows. The 'Sommerfrische' atmosphere of the Viennese bourgeoisie of the 19th century is still palpable - Archduke Johann and writer Adalbert Stifter stayed here. Alpine gardens on the opposite bank.

activities.text: Browse the weekly market on the Marktplatz for cheeses and Aufschnitt (cold cuts) from the region - authentic atmosphere, no tourist performance. The vendors speak loud and fast. It's on Tuesdays.

If the sun holds, climb to Tressenstein on the marked trail from the town centre - the Tressensteinalm and its panorama over the three lakes of Ausseerland is the region's reference viewpoint. Allow 1h30 return from the base.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Styria - Ausseerland, Gesäuse and wine country

visits.text: Hard to believe you're swimming in a painting, yet that's exactly the feeling at Rogner Bad Blumau. Hundertwasser conceived the world's largest habitable work of art here: green roofs, asymmetrical windows, coloured columns, golden domes - inaugurated 10 May 1997 across 40 hectares. More than 2,400 different windows and 330 columns.

The water from the Vulkania spring emerges at 110°C from 2,843 metres depth, cooled before feeding the thermal lake. The natural salt content is such that you float effortlessly - the sensation of swimming in the sea, according to regulars. It's the most mineralised spring in Styria's thermal region.

Rogner Bad Blumau has been voted 'Austria's most popular spa' several times by Thermencheck readers. The geothermal installation has produced electricity since 2001 - the spring serves simultaneously for heating, bathing and power generation. A circular logic that would have delighted Hundertwasser.

activities.text: Treat yourself to the Vulkania Heilquelle, the outdoor thermal lake - bathing lasts between 1h and half a day depending on your mood. The canopied rest areas and indoor galleries are designed for staying put, not circulating. Resist trying to see everything.
If you're staying overnight, book the sauna on arrival - slots fill fast at weekends. Under-16s not permitted and practised naked as in Austria. Aufgüsse (steam infusions) scheduled every hour.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Styria - Ausseerland, Gesäuse and wine country

places.name: Bad Kleinkirchheim

visits.text: Bad Kleinkirchheim is a thermal spa and ski resort at 1,100 m altitude in the Nockberge mountains. Its natural thermal springs emerge at 36°C—discovered in 1834 and used since for their healing properties. The Therme Römerbad and Therme St. Kathrein offer panoramic outdoor pools, alpine saunas, and wellness treatments with mountain views.

The Nockalm-Straße traverses the UNESCO Biosphere Park Nockberge over 35 km with 52 bends and 8 designated viewpoints. The rounded, grassy landscapes, sculpted by Quaternary glaciers, harbor over 2,000 plant species—exceptional botanical diversity linked to the Nockberge limestone.

activities.text: Thermal relaxation after the scenic drive. In winter, Bad Kleinkirchheim offers 103 km of slopes with 25 lifts and regularly hosts Alpine Ski World Cup rounds on the legendary Franz Klammer piste.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

map_keywords: Bad Kleinkirchheim thermes Römerbad St Kathrein
Bad Kleinkirchheim station ski Coupe du Monde

visits.text: Brandnertal valley in Vorarlberg, enclosed between the Rätikon and Bregenzerwaldgebirge massifs. The village of Brand at 1,037 meters is the starting point for several cable cars and access to the Lünersee. Traditional Vorarlberg chalets, alpine cheese dairies, and open panoramas.

Lünersee (1,970 m), a heart-shaped lake surrounded by limestone walls with direct views of the Schesaplana (2,965 m), the Rätikon's highest point. Classified as the 'Pearl of the Alps,' its turquoise waters contrast with the gray scree of surrounding cliffs. Complete lake loop: 6 km, 2h, easy.

Lünerseebahn from Brand to the lake in 5 minutes, cable car open mid-May through late October. The views during the ascent over the valley and Rätikon peaks are already spectacular before even reaching the lake.

activities.text: Hike toward Totalphütte (2,385 m) from Lünersee—1.5 hours additional ascent to reach this refuge facing the Schesaplana glaciers. Challenging level, mountain equipment required above 2,200 meters.

Bürser Schlucht gorges (2h route), trail on walkways and suspension bridges through limestone gorges with waterfalls and rock formations. Start from Bürs (10 min from Brand). Spectacular and accessible for everyone.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 days in Austria: Wachau Valley, Alpine Peaks & Salzburg

visits.text: The Villacher Alpenstrasse climbs 16.5 km to 1,732 meters on the Dobratsch massif, with designated viewpoints over all the Carinthian lakes. The Alpine Botanical Garden, at mid-route, catalogs over 600 high-mountain plant species. The new Rote Wand viewpoint offers exceptional panoramic views over Villach and the Karawanken range.

The hike from the terminal parking lot to the Dobratsch summit (2,166 m) takes 1.5 hours. The panoramic summit restaurant serves alpine cuisine and regional beers with all of Carinthia spread out before you in clear weather.

activities.text: Evening descent to Warmbad Villach thermal baths (KärntenTherme) for a wellness session after the day at altitude. The natural thermal springs at 29°C and the panoramic outdoor pools are particularly appreciated at sunset.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

visits.text: The Dreiländereck is the geographical meeting point of Austria, Italy, and Slovenia, accessible by chairlift from Arnoldstein in 15 minutes. From the upper station, the panorama extends simultaneously across three countries: the Carinthian Alps, the Friulian Dolomites, and Slovenian mountains.

Several hiking trails depart from the upper station: easy ridge walks or full-day hikes for the more athletic. The refuge chalet terrace serves Jausen (alpine snacks) with views of peaks in three countries.

activities.text: The Dreiländereck chairlift is included in the Kärnten Card. Open from late May to late September. The Kärnten Card provides access to over 100 Carinthian attractions—cost-effective after 4-5 uses.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

visits.text: Medieval village of Dürnstein, a true jewel of the Wachau Valley. Its cobbled lanes lined with pastel Baroque houses, fountains, and wine cellars cluster around the Augustinian Abbey, whose blue tower is the valley's emblem—visible for kilometers along the Danube.

Dürnstein Castle ruins perched 230 meters above the village. Here Richard the Lionheart was imprisoned in 1192 after the Third Crusade, held by Duke Leopold V of Austria. The ransom of 150,000 silver marks equaled twice England's annual revenue. 20-30 minute climb from the village, free access.

Melk Abbey (UNESCO), a Benedictine fortress-monastery dominating the Danube from a rocky spur 60 meters high. Founded in the 11th century, redesigned in flamboyant Baroque in the 18th, it houses a library of 100,000 volumes, a gilded church, and a panoramic terrace over the Wachau. Benedictine monks still live and work here today.

Terraced vineyards of the Wachau between Melk and Krems, 30 km of UNESCO-listed landscape since 2000. Three protected appellations: Steinfeder (light), Federspiel (medium), and Smaragd (age-worthy). The Grüner Veltliner and Riesling from these limestone slopes rank among Austria's finest white wines.

activities.text: Short Danube cruise between Dürnstein and Spitz (30 min) with DDSG or Brandner. Unique views of vineyard cliffs, castle ruins, and villages tucked into hidden coves. Available May through October, hourly departures from Dürnstein pier.

Grüner Veltliner tasting at a local winemaker's. Buschenschenken (vintner taverns marked by a pine branch) serve their own production alongside regional charcuterie and cheeses. Domäne Tegernseerhof and Weingut Knoll are among the most renowned.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 days in Austria: Wachau Valley, Alpine Peaks & Salzburg

visits.text: This baroque mausoleum is worth the detour for its two limestone giants alone - the tomb guardians sculpted by Andreas Marx around 1680 to watch over Ruprecht von Eggenberg, imperial general who died in 1611. The mausoleum, designed by Pietro de Pomis (the architect of the Graz Mausoleum), has been the property of Styria since 1920 and is a listed monument.

Ehrenhausen Castle dominates the village from a wooded hill - first mention of the fortress in 1240, then transformed into a Renaissance palace between 1543 and 1755 by the Eggenbergs. The view from the forecourt over the Mur valley and the first foothills of the Wine Road is the gateway panorama to southern Styria.

activities.text: Walk along the Mur to Traussner mill via Rundwanderweg no. 4 from Ehrenhausen centre - 1h30 return on forest and meadow paths, with constant views of the Schloss and Mausoleum from the bank. Flat terrain.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Styria - Ausseerland, Gesäuse and wine country

visits.text: Village of Ehrwald at 994 meters in the Außerfern valley, at the foot of the Wetterstein massif. Authentic Tyrolean town with its flower-balcony farmhouses, Baroque church, and direct views of the Zugspitze's north face (2,962 m). Austrian-side starting point for ascending Germany's highest summit.

Zugspitze (2,962 m) via the Tiroler Zugspitzbahn, an Austrian cable car departing from Ehrwald and reaching the summit in 10 minutes in 100-person cabins. At the top: views across 4 countries (Austria, Germany, Switzerland, Italy), Faszination Zugspitze museum, panoramic restaurant, and the Schneeferner Glacier.

Seebensee (1,657 m), a mountain lake in a rocky cirque at the foot of the Zugspitze. Accessible by hike from Ehrwald (2.5h, 650 m elevation gain) or via the Ehrwalder Almbahn. Icy green-reflecting waters, limestone boulders, alpine silence—one of the Wetterstein massif's most beautiful settings.

activities.text: Hike to Coburger Hütte (1,917 m) from Seebensee—30 additional minutes from the lake to reach this refuge with views over the Drachensee. Intermediate level, well-marked trail, refuge open June to October.

BAHNORAMA anno 1926 museum in the Zugspitzbahn cable car's lower station, tracing 100 years of alpine cable car history with models, archival documents, and period films. Entrance included with cable car ticket.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 days in Austria: Wachau Valley, Alpine Peaks & Salzburg

visits.text: Hard not to be intimidated entering the Haydnsaal of Schloss Esterházy in Eisenstadt: the acoustics have been praised since the 17th century, and the ceiling fresco of Cupid and Psyche forgives no inattention. Joseph Haydn composed within these walls from 1761 - he remained linked to the Esterházy house until his death in 1809, though his activity as kapellmeister ended around 1790. The Herbstgold festival concerts in September still take place here.

Austria's largest wine museum is in the cellar, beneath Schloss Esterházy: the Vinothek Burgenland in the basement offers more than 850 regional labels by the glass or bottle, accompanied by local tapas. Ideal for an introduction to Pannonian reds before the Rust stop.

The Bergkirche of Eisenstadt: Haydn has been buried there since 1820, after a posthumous journey that surpasses fiction. His skull was separated from his body shortly after death by admirers of phrenology and only rejoined the rest of his remains in 1954. The Haydngruft in the crypt of the Bergkirche is open year-round.

activities.text: Stroll through the old town to Dom St. Martin: the cathedral is Burgenland's most important religious building, with its Gothic stained glass and cloister. The Haydngasse, just a few steps away, leads to the Haydnhaus - the museum dedicated to the composer's Eisenstadt years, in his house from 1766.

If it's September, the Herbstgold festival occupies the Haydnsaal and the castle park with a programme mixing jazz, Balkan music and classical - it's the successor to the old Haydn Festspiele since 2017. Booking essential, tickets on esterhazy.at. Outside the festival, chamber concerts take place almost every week.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

places.name: Faaker See

visits.text: Faaker See is one of Carinthia's most beautiful lakes: its deep turquoise waters reach 27°C in summer. This 2.5-km-long lake is characterized by its small central island, which remains one of the region's most photographed images. Its modest size guarantees rapid warming and ideal swimming conditions.
The ascent to Hochhinauf-Taborhöhe offers the best plunging views of Faaker See and its island. Access via forest trails takes about 45 minutes from the lakeshore.

activities.text: Water sports equipment rental at the developed beaches of Drobollach and Egg am Faaker See: pedal boats, kayaks, paddleboards. A walking trail completely encircles the lake in 1.5 hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

map_keywords: Taborhöhe point de vue Faaker See

visits.text: Locals call it their Acropolis: the Burgruine Falkenstein on its limestone spur has dominated the Weinviertel vineyards since the mid-11th century. The fortress was built under Emperor Heinrich III as a bulwark against Bohemia and Hungary. The natural cave in the limestone and the museum of the Täuferbewegung - the Anabaptists once imprisoned here - justify the climb. Open April to October, guided tours on request.

A wooden galley in an Austrian fortress: in the first courtyard of Burgruine Falkenstein, a full-size replica of a Venetian galley recalls the fate of Anabaptists condemned to the galleys of Trieste in the 16th century. The model was built by HTL-Mödling in 2010-2011. A detail that few audioguides mention, and one that children remember effortlessly.

activities.text: Climb early: the view across the vineyards of Bohemia stretches to the Czech horizon in clear weather. The path from the village takes about twenty minutes on foot, well signposted. On Sundays at 3pm, a guided tour starts automatically with 8 or more people - no booking needed if you're in a large enough group.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: Tscheppaschlucht is one of Carinthia's most beautiful gorges. Water churns between steep rock walls, forming cascades and whirlpools. The well-equipped trail—metal walkways, ladders, stairs carved into rock—follows the river for about 2 km in 2 hours of exploration. The Teufelsbrücke (Devil's Bridge) section offers the most dramatic views of the waterfalls.

The Bodental Valley, accessible from the gorge exit, shelters the Meerauge—a small lake with intense blue-green colors and exceptionally clear water, fed by underground springs. The Märchenwiese (Fairy Meadow), a 20-minute walk away, is an alpine meadow surrounded by wooded walls of remarkable serenity.

activities.text: Combined gorge + Bodental Valley as a full-day excursion. Bring hiking shoes with non-slip soles—the Tscheppaschlucht trail is permanently wet. Picnicking possible on the Märchenwiese lawns.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

visits.text: Village of Filzmoos at 1,057 meters altitude, nestled between the twin peaks of the Bischofsmütze ('Bishop's Mitre') and the Dachstein massif. This authentic alpine hamlet in Salzburgerland has been certified as an 'Austrian Hiking Village' and offers 250 km of marked trails within a 10 km radius.

Pilgrimage Parish Church of Filzmoos, a 15th-century Gothic edifice dedicated to Saints Peter and Paul. It houses the sculpture of the Filzmooser Kindl, a colorful Gothic Christ Child surrounded by a carved golden halo—one of the village's 17 'Power Spots.'

Panorama of the Bischofsmütze (2,454 m) from the village, this twin-peaked summit dominating the entire Schladming-Dachstein region. Its silhouette recalls a bishop's mitre, hence the name. Part of the Gosaukamm massif in the Limestone Alps.

Dachstein Krippenstein cable car from Obertraun, two successive sections climbing to 2,109 meters. From the summit, panoramic views over the Limestone Alps, Lake Hallstatt, and the Traun gorges. Bring warm clothing even in summer—temperatures drop 10 to 15°C.

5 Fingers viewing platform at 2,109 meters, five steel walkways jutting into the void above a 400-meter cliff. Each has a different design—one features a transparent glass floor. From the cable car, a 20-minute walk on a marked trail.

Dachstein Ice Cave (Rieseneishöhle), a 50-minute guided tour through ice galleries formed over millennia. King Arthur's Cathedral and the Ice Palace with its 9-meter ice cone are the highlights. Permanent temperature of -2°C—jacket required.

Mammoth Cave (Mammuthöhle), one of Europe's largest caves with 70 km explored. Limestone galleries with spectacular formations carved by water over millennia. Guided tour independent of the ice cave—combination ticket recommended.

activities.text: Arrival hike to the Hofpürglhütte (1,705 m), an alpine refuge at the foot of the Bischofsmütze. From the Unterhofalm parking lot, count on 2 hours uphill for 600 meters of elevation gain. Spectacular views of the Gosaukamm massif and Dachstein. Refuge open mid-May through late October.
Papageno cable car from the village center to access high-altitude pastures and panoramic trails without effort. Starting point for numerous family hikes in summer, ski area in winter.

Hike on the Dachstein Rundwanderweg, a 3-day circuit looping around the massif. Even a half-day on the Krippenstein-Schönbergalm section offers magnificent glacier views. Well-marked trail, intermediate level.

Hallstatt (20 min from Filzmoos): Austria's most photographed lakeside village, squeezed between lake and cliffs. 7,000-year-old salt mine (UNESCO), Beinhaus (ossuary) decorated with painted skulls, and views from the Welterbeblick platform 360 meters above the lake.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 days in Austria: Wachau Valley, Alpine Peaks & Salzburg
12 days in Austria: Wachau Valley, Alpine Peaks & Salzburg

visits.text: Raggaschlucht, in Hohe Tauern National Park near Flattach, is one of Carinthia's most spectacular gorges. Wooden walkways guide visitors along the river through successive rapids and waterfalls, on an 800-meter course ascending to the main waterfall. The confined walls and vegetation of ferns and mosses create a primeval forest atmosphere.

Groppensteinschlucht, a few kilometers away, is a 2.5-km gorge with wooden trails alongside the wild river. The easy route suits families and takes 1.5 to 2 hours. In season, nighttime visits allow you to discover the beautifully illuminated gorge after dark.

activities.text: Both gorges can be visited on the same day. Waterproof or hiking shoes essential—the walkways are constantly sprayed. Open from May to October.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

visits.text: Burg Forchtenstein has belonged to the Esterházy since 1626: for four centuries, no sale, no transfer, no fire. The Zeughaus contains one of the largest private weapons collections in Central Europe, and the Schatzkammer - the only baroque Kunstkammer still in its original location in Europe - astonishes with its density. Free entrance with the Burgenland Card.

Burgenland locals say: the most beautiful view of the flatlands from the Rosaliengebirge. From the ramparts of Burg Forchtenstein on its dolomite rock, the gaze descends across the Pannonian plain as far as Hungary in clear weather. In the inner courtyard, the secco mural frescoes are described as the largest of their kind north of the Alps.

activities.text: Don't miss the Schatzkammer: the Esterházy treasure chamber - accessible with a combined ticket - presents hundreds of baroque objects in their original cabinets, never moved since the 17th century. The monthly 'Nachtwache', in which Franciscus Fabiankovich, former fortress captain in 1683, comes back to life, is the most memorable visit.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: The Linzertor: 28 metres high, one of the most imposing gate towers in Central Europe. At the foot of the gate, the two long slits in the masonry were used to manoeuvre the beams of the drawbridge portcullis. The Habsburg imperial eagle sits alongside the Babenberg shield - the town's coat of arms. Freistadt was one of the seven state cities of Upper Austria.

Freistadt's Braucommune: communism, certainly, but of beer. Since the Middle Ages, the brewing right has been reserved for homeowners within the walled town - and is transferred with the walls. Today, some forty 'Braukommunisten' collectively own the baroque brewery built in the 18th century. The Bierakademie offers sommelier training courses.

activities.text: Join the Nachtwächter on Fridays at 8pm from the Gästeinfo at Hauptplatz 1: Freistadt's official night watchman guides visitors through the Linzertor to the corner towers and medieval cellars in period costume. Lasts about 1h30, in German, around 8 euros. Booking not mandatory, but capacity is limited.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: Friesach is Carinthia's oldest town, documented since 860 AD, and southern Austria's best-preserved medieval city. Its historic center remains surrounded by virtually intact 12th-century ramparts stretching 1.8 kilometers—a rarity in the Alps. The Hauptplatz is lined with Gothic and Renaissance houses, many retaining their original arcades and medieval inner courtyards.

Petersberg Castle, erected by the Bishops of Salzburg in the 12th century on the hill overlooking town, houses the municipal museum. Archaeological collections, sacred art, and medieval life reconstructions trace the history of this town that was, for centuries, Carinthia's most important.

The 12th-century Romanesque parish church preserves its Gothic frescoes and carved wooden altar. The adjacent Dominican monastery, founded in 1217, is one of the oldest German-language monasteries still in operation.

activities.text: Friesach hosts a unique experimental archaeology project: the reconstruction of a medieval castle using exclusively 13th-century techniques and tools. Visitors can watch craftsmen at work and understand how fortresses were built 800 years ago.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

visits.text: The hills of Gamlitz smell of must in autumn and resin in summer - it's Styria's largest wine-growing commune with around 550 hectares of vines and some 180 estates. Sauvignon Blanc from southern Styria has found its sharpest expression here.

The Wine Museum at Gamlitz Castle (Weinbaumuseum Schloss Gamlitz) traces the history of Styrian viticulture from Celtic and Roman times to current techniques. The old castle cellar allows for on-site tasting. Small museum, but honest.

activities.text: Take the Weinbergbummler, the little tourist train that winds between the vines of Gamlitzberg and Sernauberg on the South Styrian Wine Road. The 3h30 loop includes a cellar visit with tasting. Departure from Gamlitz.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Styria - Ausseerland, Gesäuse and wine country

visits.text: Founded in 1153 by Count Ulrich von Pernegg, Stift Geras houses in its Marmorsaal the only Paul Troger ceiling fresco that has never been restored - an absolute rarity in the region. The Premonstratensian monastery survived the Hussites, the Swedes and the Soviets: no war destroyed it, only fires transformed it. Open May-October, Thursday-Sunday, entrance 7 euros without guide.

In the Naturpark Geras adjacent to the abbey, reconstituted aurochs graze freely on 132 hectares of forest and meadow - an extinct species whose genetic recreation programme produces animals surprisingly close to their ancestors. An observation platform allows you to see lynx, deer and heron without a guide. Open from Palm Sunday to 31 October.

activities.text: Visit the abbey's medicinal plant garden, which perpetuates the work of Kräuterpfarrer Hermann Weidinger - the canon who popularised herbal medicine in Austria in the 1970s. The garden is freely accessible from May to October. The shop sells in-house preparations: Waldviertel herbal teas are among the best local labels to take home.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: The Naturpark Blockheide alone justifies the detour to Gmünd: granite blocks weighing several tonnes have been balancing on their pedestals for millennia, sculpted by chemical erosion in a moorland of heather and pines. These Wackelsteine are not glacial erratics but 'Restlinge' - a local distinction that Austrians defend with conviction.

The rocking stones (Wackelsteine) of granite are called the Teufelsbett, the Christophorusstein or the Koboldsteine according to local legends. The nature park is crossed by the 15th meridian east of Greenwich - a sign in the middle of the moorland marks it. The 30-metre panoramic tower at the Infozentrum offers a view as far as Czechia.

activities.text: Climb the 30 metres of the Naturpark Blockheide panoramic tower from the Informationszentrum - the ascent begins through the interior of the old 1908 water reservoir. Entrance 3.50 euros, free with the NÖ Card. Four themed trails start from there, from 2.9 to 5.6 km, with the Marienkäferweg offering the most complete panorama over the Blockheide.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: The waters of Vorderer Gosausee shimmer emerald green - the Dachstein massif (2,995 m) reflects in it like an eye. Gosau locals call this lake 'the eye of God' and, seen from here, you understand why. The lake can be walked around in 45 minutes on a flat path, accessible May to October. Park at Parkplatz Vorderer Gosausee and arrive early morning to beat the tour buses.

The Gosaukammbahn cable car climbs from the lake to Zwieselalm, a panoramic alpine pasture on the Gosaukamm ridge. At the top, the ridge trail offers 360° views over the Dachstein and Gosaukamm (2,458 m at Große Bischofsmütze). Times vary by season - generally open May to October.

activities.text: Walk around the lake in 45 minutes on the Rundwanderweg, then if you have time continue on foot to Hinterer Gosausee - 30 minutes extra on a forest path. This second lake is wilder and far less crowded than its sibling.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Styria - Ausseerland, Gesäuse and wine country

visits.text: Graz's Old Town (Grazer Altstadt), a UNESCO World Heritage site, unfolds its baroque and Renaissance façades around Hauptplatz—the central main square. Hidden interior courtyards, sloping cobblestone lanes, and discreet passages form a medieval labyrinth renewed at every turn. Head to Herrengasse for its arcaded houses and sculpted façades.

The Schlossberg is the wooded hill dominating Graz from its center, once crowned by a medieval fortress dismantled by Napoleon in 1809. Only the Clock Tower (Uhrturm) remains, the city's emblem, whose clock has displayed time backwards since 1712: the big hand shows hours. Ascend by funicular or 260 steps—the view over Graz's red roofs and bell towers is panoramic.

The Kunsthaus Graz, affectionately nicknamed the "Friendly Alien," is a bluish bubble with a spaceship look perched on the Mur riverbanks. Designed by Peter Cook and Colin Fournier in 2003 for Graz's bid as European Capital of Culture, it presents contemporary art exhibitions with spectacular installations. Even children remain fascinated by its organic shapes and "nozzles"—those porthole-antennas oriented toward the city.

The Styrian Armoury (Landeszeughaus) preserves over 32,000 suits of armor, swords, pikes, and cannonballs—Austria's largest collection of historic armor. Entire halls of 16th-century ceremonial armor, still arranged as if their owners might return, create an impressive spectacle. Perfect for captivating children with tales of chivalry.

activities.text: Climb to the Schlossberg summit by funicular, and once at the top, spot the giant underground slide carved through rock that takes visitors back down to the Old Town. An unusual attraction loved by children, tunneled through the hill's rocky core.

Stroll through the markets at Kaiser-Josef-Platz and Hauptplatz, where Styrian producers display fruits, vegetables, cheeses, and the essential Kürbiskernöl—green pumpkin seed oil, a regional specialty with a roasted hazelnut flavor, drizzled over salads and cheeses.

days_plan.itin_title: Family Road Trip Through Austria: Mountains, Lakes & Imperial Palaces

visits.text: Three lakes, one journey - simple in theory, except that Toplitzsee can only be reached by traditional flat-bottomed boat (Plätte) or on foot. The Kammersee, right at the end, is the source of the River Traun. Schifffahrt Grundlsee offers the '3-Seen-Tour' from May to late October: nostalgic motor boat on Grundlsee, walk (1.6 km) to Toplitzsee, then Plätte.

Toplitzsee has no dissolved oxygen below 20 metres depth - a particular chemistry that fuelled decades of rumours about Nazi treasure hidden in its waters. Divers did find crates, but no gold. Real relics are on display at the Fischerhütte on the lake shore.

activities.text: Board the Schifffahrt Grundlsee from opening time - the crossing of Grundlsee takes 30 minutes, time enough to see the reflections of the Totes Gebirge. Allow minimum 3h30 for the three-lake loop. Departures several times daily May to October, tickets online or at the pier.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Styria - Ausseerland, Gesäuse and wine country

visits.text: Gurk Cathedral, consecrated in 1200, is considered Austria's most important Romanesque religious building. Its facade with twin 44-meter towers and its interior covered with over 1,000 m² of Romanesque and Gothic frescoes make it an exceptional monument. The crypt, supported by 100 columns with all-different carved capitals, houses the sarcophagus of Blessed Emma—one of the purest examples of 11th-century Romanesque architecture in Central Europe.

The Diocesan Museum, in the adjacent episcopal palace, displays one of Austria's richest collections of medieval sacred art: liturgical vestments, gold reliquaries, and the 12th-century Chalice of Gurk with cloisonné enamels. The library preserves illuminated manuscripts from the 13th-15th centuries.

activities.text: Michael Hönel's baroque altar (1740) dominates the cathedral choir. The Gurk Treasury, displayed in an adjacent room, gathers seven centuries of sacred goldwork—a visit that perfectly complements the Romanesque cathedral.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

visits.text: Seven thousand years of salt mining: the Salzwelten Hallstatt is the oldest known salt mine in the world. The wooden staircase discovered inside dates from 1344 BCE - the oldest in Europe. Note: the mine is closed for renovation until 30 June 2026; a free shuttle to Salzwelten Altaussee is offered as an alternative. Book early.

Tiny village, massive crowds - and yet Hallstatt remains beautiful. The Marktplatz and its houses clinging to the cliff face, squeezed between lake and rock, have been UNESCO-listed since 1997. Arrive by boat from Obertraun for the most cinematic entrance.

activities.text: Take the funicular early to avoid queues - the view from the Welterbeblick, 350 metres above the lake, is dizzying. Coming back down, walk along the east side of Hallstätter See: the raking morning light on the houses is worth the detour.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Styria - Ausseerland, Gesäuse and wine country

visits.text: The moat reflects the four round towers of Burg Heidenreichstein like a still mirror: no other fortress in Austria produces this effect. The Wasserburg dates from the 12th century, belongs to the Kinsky family in the form of a forestry company and remains partly a private residence. The walls of the main building reach four metres thick - which partly explains its resilience.

Never conquered since the 12th century: besieged three times, Burg Heidenreichstein never suffered significant military damage - a rare case among Austria's medieval fortresses. Access is still via two working drawbridges, which the guides operate during tours. Visits Tuesday-Sunday at 9:30am, 11am, 2pm and 3:30pm.

activities.text: Interior visits are by guided tour only - no exceptions. The Kinsky family guides comment on each room with the precision of local historians. A separate ticket allows access to the underground secret passage. Closed Mondays - plan your arrival for another day.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: The Grossglockner High Alpine Road (Grossglockner Hochalpenstraße) is one of the world's most beautiful scenic routes. This 48-km itinerary winds through Hohe Tauern National Park with 36 designated viewpoints, reaching Edelweiss Pass at 2,571 m. Built between 1930 and 1935, it remains one of Austrian civil engineering's great masterpieces.

Kaiser-Franz-Josefs-Höhe offers a direct view of the Pasterze Glacier (8.4 km long, the largest glacier in the Eastern Alps) and Grossglockner (3,798 m, Austria's highest peak). Wild marmots, ibex, and golden eagles inhabit the viewpoint surroundings. A funicular descends to the glacier front.

Heiligenblut, at the road's foot on the Carinthian side, is a classically beautiful alpine village with its Gothic church and pointed steeple framed by glaciers. The Jungfernsprung waterfall (130 m), visible from the village, takes its name from a medieval legend.

activities.text: The road is toll (car rate). Open from May to November depending on snowfall. Bring warm clothing even in summer—the temperature difference between valley and pass can exceed 15°C. The Mallnitz visitor center presents the National Park's fauna and flora.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

visits.text: More than 300 bird species, light that flattens the world: the Nationalpark Neusiedler See-Seewinkel is one of the few parks in Europe to simultaneously host white storks, imperial eagles, avocets and great bustards. Founded in 1993 on the Austrian side, UNESCO-listed in 2001, it extends over about 10,000 ha. The Informationszentrum in Illmitz is open year-round.

The pink Salzlacken in autumn: end of the world. These periodically dried salt lagoons of the Seewinkel change colour with the season - halophilic algae tint them salmon pink in summer and autumn. The Zicklacke between Illmitz and Apetlon is the most accessible. The old Hungarian observation tower at Sandeck in Illmitz offers the best viewpoint over the lake.

activities.text: Rent a bike in Illmitz, the lake path is flat and the Neusiedlersee-Radweg circles the entire lake in six stages over 121 km. The Illmitz-Rust section is ideal for a half-day loop. The curious will go down to the edge of the Zicklacke at dusk: the spectacle of greylag geese heading to their roosts is one of those things you don't forget.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: Golden Roof (Goldenes Dachl), a covered balcony with 2,657 fire-gilded copper tiles built in 1500 for Emperor Maximilian I. This imperial box above the old town allowed the emperor to watch tournaments from his palace. It's Innsbruck's absolute emblem.

Innsbruck Old Town (Altstadt), a preserved medieval ensemble between the Golden Roof and the Nordkette. Cobbled lanes, colorful Baroque-fronted houses, Herzog-Friedrich Street with its Gothic arcades, and the exuberant 18th-century Baroque stucco Helbling House.

Hofburg Palace Innsbruck, the Habsburgs' summer residence rebuilt by Maria Theresa in the 18th century in late Baroque style. The Giant Hall with life-size portraits of the empress's 22 children is particularly impressive. Audio guide included with admission.

Nordkettenbahn, funicular and cable cars designed by Zaha Hadid linking Innsbruck's center to Seegrube (1,905 m) then Hafelekar (2,256 m) in 30 minutes from the old town. Complete aerial views over Innsbruck and the Inn Valley from the Karwendel peaks.

Court Church (Hofkirche), Maximilian I's mausoleum with his cenotaph surrounded by 28 two-meter bronze statues nicknamed the 'black fellows.' These bronze giants represent ancestors, allies, and legendary figures of the Habsburg house.

Ambras Castle (4 km south of Innsbruck), Italian Renaissance built by Archduke Ferdinand II in the 1560s. Houses three museums: the armor collection (400 pieces, one of Europe's finest), the Wunderkammer (chamber of wonders), and the Habsburg portrait gallery. The Spanish Hall (43 m long) is one of Austria's most important Renaissance interiors.

Swarovski Crystal Worlds (Kristallwelten) in Wattens, 20 km east of Innsbruck. Founded in 1895, Swarovski factory creates a cultural park around crystal with giant art installations, sculpture gardens, and museum boutique. Shuttle from Innsbruck in 30 min.

Tyroleans say of Innsbruck that it's 'too small for a metropolis, too large for a village' - and they see this as an asset. Capital of the Land of Tyrol, it crosses on foot in twenty minutes while offering museums, an old town, and the Karwendel Alps as backdrop. Arrive by train: the panorama on arrival.

The Goldenes Dachl, a balcony with 2,657 gilded copper tiles, was built in 1500 by Maximilian I to observe tournaments from his imperial box. The excess is intentional: 2,657 tiles for a balcony. The small museum inside is worth the visit, 20 minutes suffice.

The Hofburg in Innsbruck doesn't have the scope of Vienna's, but concentrates in a few rooms the essence of the Habsburgs' presence in Tyrol. The Hall of Giants gathers portraits of several generations of the imperial family. Book in the morning to avoid school groups.

activities.text: Ascent to Bergisel, an Olympic ski jump designed by Zaha Hadid (2002) with a 50-meter panoramic tower. Breathtaking views over Innsbruck and the Alps from the glass tower. Summit restaurant accessible by elevator. Two Olympic Games were held here (1964 and 1976).

Alpine Zoo Bergisel (2,000 alpine arc animals, Europe's highest zoo at 750 m), accessible from Bergisel. Wolves, lynx, brown bears, and golden eagles in enclosures open to alpine panoramas. Included with Innsbruck Card.

Hike on the Nockspitze (2,404 m) from the Mutteralmbahn station (1,620 m) above Mutters/Natters, accessible from Innsbruck by tram and bus. 800 meters additional elevation gain, unparalleled panorama over Innsbruck. Intermediate to challenging level.

Innsbruck gastronomy in restaurants and Wirtshäuser (inns) in the historic center. Must-tries: Apfelstrudel at Strudelcafé Kröll (Innrain), Tiroler Gröstl (potato, bacon, and egg skillet), and Speckknödel (bacon dumplings) washed down with local Stiegl Brewery beer.

You'll walk up the Maria-Theresien-Strasse to the baroque Annasäule column, erected in 1706 to celebrate Tyrol's defense. The pedestrian street is lined with Venetian baroque facades with the Karwendel Alps visible to the north. Step back from the fountain to frame it all.

The smell of Kiachl, Tyrolean fritters fried in lard, wafts from markets in autumn and winter. During the Christkindlmarkt, the old town smells of cinnamon and Glühwein. Outside the holidays, local bakeries serve their specialties from 7am.

days_plan.itin_title: Family Road Trip Through Austria: Mountains, Lakes & Imperial Palaces
12 days in Austria: Wachau Valley, Alpine Peaks & Salzburg
12 days in Austria: Wachau Valley, Alpine Peaks & Salzburg
Grand Alpine Traverse, 16 days: Grisons to Trieste

visits.text: The master is unknown; the masterpiece, obvious. The Kefermarkter Flügelaltar of the Pfarrkirche St. Wolfgang was commissioned in 1490 by Christoph von Zelking and completed around 1497 - but no archive names its author. Adalbert Stifter saved it from woodworm in 1852-1855 during a restoration that also removed all traces of the original polychromy. A pity.

The neo-Gothic altar of Kefermarkt reaches 13.4 metres high and 6.3 metres wide, carved from linden wood by an unknown master. The Pfarrkirche of Kefermarkt also houses two baroque side altars with canvases attributed to Altomonte and a pupil of Rubens. The church is open every day all year round, with a coin-operated info cassette for self-guided tours.

activities.text: Then climb up to Schloss Weinberg opposite, on the promontory: the late Renaissance castle houses a museum and the Schlossbrauerei Weinberg in the converted former stables. Beer is brewed on site, the Aststube offers solid regional cuisine. Castle visit itself costs 8 euros, brewery freely accessible.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

places.name: Kitzeck Im Sausal

visits.text: Winemakers say the morning fog here makes all the difference - Kitzeck im Sausal is Europe's highest wine village according to local sources, at over 560 metres. The vines clinging to the steep Sausal slopes produce Welschrieslings of particular acidity.

The 1st Styrian Wine Museum (1. Steirisches Weinmuseum), opened in 1979 in an early-18th-century listed Winzerhaus (vintner's house), brings together over 1,000 tools and artefacts related to Styrian viticulture. Entry €3. Guided tours by reservation at weekends.

activities.text: Taste a Welschriesling at a local Buschenschank - these vintners' taverns open 'when the pine branch is hung on the door' serve ham, local cheese and rye bread. No written menu, no menu board, just whatever's available that day.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Styria - Ausseerland, Gesäuse and wine country

map_keywords: Kitzeck im Sausal Wine Museum Styria

visits.text: Klagenfurt am Wörthersee, capital of Carinthia, revolves around the Alter Platz—its main square flanked by Italian-style Renaissance and Art Nouveau facades. The Dragon Fountain (Lindwurmbrunnen, 1590) commands the center: this mythical creature has been the city's emblem since medieval times, when locals believed a dragon inhabited the marshes where Klagenfurt was founded.

The Landhaus, a 16th-century Renaissance masterpiece, houses the Hall of Coats of Arms with 665 hand-painted shields representing Carinthia's noble families. Its inner courtyard, with double-tiered arcaded galleries, ranks among Austria's most elegant Renaissance spaces.
The MMKK (Museum Moderner Kunst Kärnten), housed in Klagenfurt Castle, presents one of Austria's most important contemporary art collections, spanning early modernism to cutting-edge installations.

Minimundus, on the shores of Lake Wörth, displays over 160 miniature replicas at 1:25 scale of the world's most famous landmarks. The Eiffel Tower, Taj Mahal, Colosseum, and Sagrada Família stand alongside Austrian monuments in meticulously maintained gardens.

Wildenstein Waterfall, in southern Carinthia near Rosen, provides a natural conclusion to the grand Carinthian loop. Accessible in a 20-minute climb from the parking area, a wooden observation platform offers direct views of the waterfall plunging down the rock face. Entry is free.

activities.text: Evening lakeside stroll along the Wörthersee promenade from the city center. The walkway stretches for kilometers along the lake, with views of the Carinthian Alps rising behind Belle Époque villas built by the Austro-Hungarian aristocracy.
Return to Klagenfurt (80 km, 1 hour) to complete the circuit. Wildenstein Waterfall, at the midpoint, serves as an ideal final stop before reaching Klagenfurt airport or train station.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles
Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

places.name: Klopeiner See

visits.text: Klopeiner See is Central Europe's warmest lake: its waters reach 29°C in summer, thanks to its shallow depth (14 m maximum) and sun exposure. This 1.8-km-long lake with turquoise waters is surrounded by wooded hills and offers well-equipped beaches and facilities around its entire perimeter.

The climb to the Kitzelberg summit, on the western shore, rewards you after a 45-minute rocky trail with a 360° panorama of the lake's turquoise waters and surrounding Carinthian mountains. It's the best viewpoint over the lake.

activities.text: Swimming, kayaking, paddleboarding, and windsurfing from Klopeiner See's developed beaches. Water sports rental companies operate on both main shores. Cycling trails around the lake allow you to circle it in two hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

map_keywords: Kitzelberg point de vue Klopeiner See

places.name: Kreuzeck

visits.text: The Kreuzeckbahn is one of Austria's last rack railways, departing from Spittal an der Drau to climb in minutes to the alpine pastures of the Kreuzeck massif. This historic railway offers a nostalgic journey through spectacular alpine landscapes, accessible to all without special equipment.

Spittal an der Drau, at the cable car base, possesses one of Austria's most beautiful Renaissance palaces: Schloss Porcia (16th century), with its inner courtyard featuring three superimposed arcaded galleries. The district museum it houses traces Upper Carinthia's history.

activities.text: Family-friendly hiking from Kreuzeckbahn's upper station on marked trails between alpine pastures and forests. Views extend to Lakes Millstätter See and Weissensee below. The Kreuzeckbahn is included in the Kärnten Card.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

map_keywords: Kreuzeckbahn chemin de fer crémaillère Spittal
Spittal an der Drau old town

visits.text: Village of Lech am Arlberg at 1,450 meters, founded in the 13th century by Walser shepherds from the Swiss Valais. Their traditional wooden houses with steep roofs still characterize the village, voted most beautiful village in Europe in 2004. The Lech torrent flows through the center under stone bridges.

Formarinsee (1,789 m), a heart-shaped lake voted Austria's most beautiful place in 2015 by Heute magazine readers. Turquoise waters surrounded by alpine meadows and 2,500-meter peaks. Accessible by hike from Lech (2h, 350 m elevation gain) or by cable car to Rüfikopf then 45 min on foot.

Rüfikopf cable car (2,362 m) offering 360° panoramas over the Arlberg massif, the Vorarlberg Alps, and, in clear weather, the Säntis massif in Switzerland. Mountain restaurant with terrace, starting point for numerous hikes into protected areas.

You'll arrive in Lech via the Arlberg road that opens onto a village of a few hundred permanent residents and dozens of four- and five-star hotels. A discreet resort reserved for selective clientele for several decades. Limited parking: arrive early or take the shuttle from Langen.

A luxury village without the usual glitz, Lech manages to stay discreet despite having one of the most selective clienteles in the Alps. The baroque village church is open during the day, often empty. Best period: March, spring snow and more accessible rates.

activities.text: Hike on the Arlberg Trail (40 km linking Lech, Zürs, Stuben, St. Christoph, and St. Anton)—even a day on the Lech-Zürs section (8 km, 2h) offers exceptional panoramas over alpine pastures and glaciers. Red-white markings, intermediate level.

Skyspace Lech, a James Turrell contemporary art installation integrated into the landscape. This contemplation space plays with natural light and sky perception through a zenithal opening. 45-minute guided tours in the evening for twilight effects.

If you're here in winter, the Arlberg domain connected to Saint Anton is one of Austria's largest. In summer, the Rüfikopf accessible by cable car overlooks the Vorarlberg ridges. Check lift schedules, variable by season.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 days in Austria: Wachau Valley, Alpine Peaks & Salzburg
Grand Alpine Traverse, 16 days: Grisons to Trieste

visits.text: Seggau Castle, built from the 12th century by the archbishops of Salzburg on a wooded hill above Leibnitz, became the seat of the bishops of Seckau until 1786. The Römersteinwand (Roman Stone Wall) within the castle walls - a wall made of Roman stones recovered in the 19th century - is one of the most important Roman lapidaries in Europe outside Italy. The great historic bell of Styria, the 'Seggauer Liesl', is still rung by hand every Sunday at noon.

The Römersteinwand set into the castle wall reveals the site's Roman past: Flavia Solva, a Roman town whose remains lie in the plain below, has left its most visible traces here. Archaeologists date the first occupation of the site to the late 4th century AD.

activities.text: Leave via Graz (50 km, A9 motorway) - GRZ airport is 15 minutes south of the centre. You can also have a glass of Seggauer Wein at the castle tavern before departing: the Stift's wine estate produces its own cuvées.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Styria - Ausseerland, Gesäuse and wine country

visits.text: A road that's also a state border: for 2.5 km, the South Styrian Wine Road runs exactly along the boundary between Austria and Slovenia. Built in 1955, part of its route then ran through Yugoslav territory. Today the Schengen zone erases customs, but Slovenian vines sit metres from Austrian ones.

The Grenztisch 'Miza Na Meji' (Border Table), installed in June 2013 directly on the frontier, is an artist's table that literally lets you sit astride two countries. An Austro-Slovenian initiative, it symbolises the reconciliation of two wine regions long separated.

activities.text: Explore the Heiligengeistklamm, a gorge carved by the stream of the same name a few kilometres south of Leutschach. The forest path is closed to traffic - on foot only. Bring your passport: the Heiligengeistkirche (Sveti Duh) that gives the gorge its name is on the Slovenian side.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Styria - Ausseerland, Gesäuse and wine country

visits.text: Templars here? Historians doubt, but the architectural evidence pleases sceptics: an underground worship hall with calculated acoustics, stonemasons' marks on the ceiling, octahedra in the knights' hall. Burg Lockenhaus, founded around 1200, lets each visitor form their own opinion. The Tempelritter-Führung lasts an extra hour on top of the entrance ticket.

The Wimperfledermaus colony scratches the vaults of Burg Lockenhaus at night: it's the largest colony of Geoffroy's bat in Central Europe, documented in a permanent exhibition. From April to July, guides conduct nocturnal visits specifically for these bats - a surprising experience in the cellars.

activities.text: Choose the Tempelritter thematic visit: a specialist guide takes visitors through the architectural clues of the Order's passage. It departs by reservation for groups, or on fixed dates posted on ritterburg.at. The Burgtaverne serves a medieval Raubritteressen in the evening - reservation essential.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: The Lunzer See, cold even in August, class 1 water - it's the only natural lake in Lower Austria, and locals call it 'their sea'. Its maximum depth reaches 34 metres. The loop trail takes about 1h30 along a forest path with sections offering direct views of the water. Swimming possible in the northern part, accessible by bus from the village.

The only natural lake in Lower Austria, the Lunzer See measures 1,600 metres long by 500 metres wide. Surrounded by the Ybbstaler Alpen and the foothills of the Ötscher, it's an ideal starting point for the Naturpark Ötscher-Tormäuer - with its gorges known as 'the Austrian Grand Canyon'. The Hammerherrenmuseum in the village traces the metallurgical history of the valley.

activities.text: A rowing boat on the lake, if the wind allows: several operators at the landing stage offer hourly rentals. The Ybbstalradweg follows the river for 107 km from Waidhofen - the Waidhofen-Lunz section is the most spectacular. From Lunz, the gorge sequence of the Ötschergräben can be explored in a 4-hour walk from Gaming.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: Since 1146, the Order of Hospitallers - which took the name Knights of Malta in 1530 - has held Schloss Mailberg: it's the oldest property of the Order in Austria, and one of the few in continuous operation. The 15th-century Kunigundenkirche chapel, overlooking the cemetery, offers a panorama across the village and the hills of the Pulkautal. Guided tours by reservation, courtyard free to enter.

The Kellergasse Zipf of Mailberg: twenty lime-whitewashed wine presses lined up on the hillside, listed as a historic monument since 1984 - the only entirely protected Kellergasse in Austria. Plunging view of the castle from the top of the path. On weekday afternoons, a few doors are open for direct tasting at the winemakers'.

activities.text: If the Schlosshotel Mailberg's wine cellar is open, that's where you'll find the best bottles of the Pulkautal at estate prices. Local reds and whites, presented in a vaulted room overlooking the moat. Open to walk-in visitors, not just hotel residents. Hours vary by season: call ahead.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: The Malta Hochalmstrasse is one of Carinthia's most beautiful scenic roads over 14.4 km. The route follows a succession of hydroelectric dams wedged between vertical walls, with waterfalls cascading from upper glaciers. Traffic lights regulate traffic in the narrowest sections.

At the road's terminus, Kölbrein Dam is Austria's highest dam (200 m tall). A glass-floored observation platform cantilevered over the void gives vertiginous views of the lower basin. The hike to Osnabrücker Hütte refuge (2.5 hours one-way) crosses a high hanging valley with waterfalls and scree.

activities.text: The road is toll (€6 per person, free with Kärnten Card). Open from May to late October. The ascent to the dam can be replaced by the parallel hiking trail for walking enthusiasts.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

visits.text: Village of Mayrhofen at the head of the Zillertal valley, surrounded by the Penken (1,790 m) and Ahorn (2,000 m) massifs. This Tyrolean village with its flower-decked chalets is the gateway to four mountain areas. The main street Hauptstraße concentrates equipment shops, restaurants, and Heuriger (wine taverns).

Penkenbahn, a modern 3S cable car (2015) linking the village to Ahorn station (2,000 m) in 10 minutes. Cabins hold 32 passengers, 1,000 meters of elevation, 3 km of cable. At the summit: panoramas over the Zillertal, the Hintertux glaciers, and peaks of the Zillertal Alps reaching 3,510 meters.

Stilluptal, a wild valley starting from Mayrhofen via a toll road (€5). Protected nature reserve featuring the Stillupstausee reservoir (80 million m³), waterfalls, and forest trails. Perfect for a gentle forest hike with dam views.

Excursion to Hintertux and its permanent glacier (3,250 m), Austria's only year-round skiable glacier. In summer, you can walk on the ice, explore marked crevasses, and enjoy panoramas over the Zillertal Alps from 3,250 meters. Cable car from the head of the Tuxertal valley.

Hintertux Glacier Cave (Gletschereis- und Tropfsteinhöhle), accessible next to the cable car. Ice stalactites and stalagmites in a network of galleries carved into the glacier. Temperature -2°C, 45-minute guided tour, spectacular lighting.

A popular ski resort, Mayrhofen is also worth visiting in summer: the Zillertal landscapes offer some of Austria's most photographed alpine pastures. Outside peak season, the valley villages return to their slow rhythm. Arrive in May or September to avoid crowds.

The milky green waters of the Ziller rush between granite banks covered in moss and rhododendrons. The alpine meadows have that smell of fresh grass and mountain manure that announces high-altitude chalets. Hike to the Ginzling Alm: 2h one-way, marked trail.

Zillertal locals call their glacier 'the Hintertuxer,' like a well-known neighbor. This permanent glacier accessible from Hintertux is skiable year-round, at over 3,000 meters. Plan half a day and check rates on hintertuxer-gletscher.at.

activities.text: Penken panoramic circuit (4.4 km) from the upper station, a trail suitable for strollers and families looping around alpine meadows with views over the four Zillertal valleys. Duration 1.5 hours, minimal elevation gain, several refuges along the route.

Gold Mine of Zell am Ziller, 10 minutes by car—tour of underground galleries from a 16th-century gold mine. Journey by small mining train followed by an exhibition on extraction history. Open May-October.

Hike to Schlegeisspeicher (1,782 m), an alpine reservoir at the head of the Zillertal Grund accessible via a scenic road from Ginzling (20 km from Mayrhofen). Turquoise lake surrounded by peaks over 3,000 meters, easy trails around the lake. Parking €8.

Erlebnisbad Mayrhofen, an aquatic complex with indoor pool, water slides, wellness area, and heated outdoor pool with mountain views. Ideal for uncertain weather or an evening of relaxation after hiking.

You'll reach the Hintertux glacier by driving up the valley from Mayrhofen to Hintertux. The cable car climbs in several sections to over 3,000 meters. Dress warm even in July: at the summit, it's several degrees below zero.

The walk from Mayrhofen to Finkenberg follows the Zillertal valley floor in 45 minutes and opens access to several high-altitude trails. Starting point for the local via ferrata as well. Best window: late June to mid-September.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 days in Austria: Wachau Valley, Alpine Peaks & Salzburg
12 days in Austria: Wachau Valley, Alpine Peaks & Salzburg
Grand Alpine Traverse, 16 days: Grisons to Trieste

visits.text: Perched on a rocky spur plunging directly into the Danube, Melk's Benedictine Abbey is one of European baroque's masterpieces. Built between 1702 and 1736 by Jakob Prandtauer, its ochre façade and gilded domes are visible for several kilometers. Since 1089—over 930 years—Benedictine monks have lived here continuously under St. Benedict's rule. The library with its 100,000 volumes, featuring a ceiling painted by Paul Troger, ranks among the world's most beautiful baroque libraries. The abbey church, imperial apartments, and gardens with three-hundred-year-old lindens complete a ninety-minute to two-hour visit.

Dürnstein, a small village of 870 inhabitants on the Danube shore, is famous for two reasons: its cobalt-blue baroque bell tower—nicknamed "God's finger"—that has signaled boats since the 18th century, and the medieval castle ruins perched above the village where Richard the Lionheart was imprisoned by Leopold V of Austria in 1192–1193 after the crusade. The climb to the ruins (30 to 45 minutes) rewards with an exceptional view over the Danube loop and Wachau vineyards.

The Wachau Valley, a UNESCO World Heritage site since 2000, is a 30-km stretch between Krems and Melk where the Danube winds through terraced vineyards and medieval villages. Wachau's Grüner Veltliner and Riesling rank among Austria's most renowned white wines. Each village hides its own interior courtyards, cobbled lanes, and fortification ruins.

activities.text: Short Danube river cruise through the Wachau. Boats navigate between wine-growing villages, pass beneath Dürnstein's ruins, and glide along terraces of apricot trees and orchards that define the region's gastronomic reputation. The Wachau apricot, protected by a European controlled designation of origin, is transformed into schnapps, jams, and traditional dumplings.

Bike ride on the Danube Cycle Path (Donauradweg) running along both river banks through the Wachau. The flat, well-maintained route crosses vineyards, orchards, and historic villages at a peaceful pace. Bike rental shops are available in Melk and Krems.

days_plan.itin_title: Family Road Trip Through Austria: Mountains, Lakes & Imperial Palaces

visits.text: Millstätter See (11.5 km long, 141 m deep) is Carinthia's second-largest lake and one of its most pristine. Surrounded by mountains exceeding 2,000 m and forests of fir and beech reflecting in crystalline water, it supplies drinking water to local communities without treatment—an indicator of its exceptional purity.

Millstatt Benedictine Abbey, founded in 1070, is one of Austria's most important Romanesque monastic complexes. The 12th-century cloister presents carved capitals depicting unique biblical and mythological scenes. The library preserves over 30,000 volumes including medieval illuminated manuscripts. Each summer, the abbey church hosts the Millstatt International Concerts.

activities.text: Cable car ascent to Goldeck (2,142 m) for hikes with panoramic views of the lake from the ridges. The Goldeck Panoramastrasse offers a driving alternative to 1,900 m. Swimming at the lake beaches in late afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

places.name: Molltaler Gletscher

visits.text: Mölltal Glacier, at 3,122 m altitude on the Carinthia-Salzburg border, is accessible by a unique combination: an underground cable car (Glacier Express) carved through the mountain, followed by a second cable car. Around the glacier, intensely turquoise lakes contrast with gray rock walls and blue ice.

activities.text: Guided glacier hike in summer on marked trails around the glacier. Views of the Hohe Tauern peaks from 3,000 m are among the most spectacular accessible without mountaineering. The cable car is included in the Kärnten Card.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

map_keywords: Mölltal Glacier cable car underground Carinthia

visits.text: Nassfeld-Pressegger See is a mountain region on the Austrian-Italian border, an outdoor sports paradise in all seasons. Pressegger See, a natural thermal lake at 560 m altitude, offers swimming in warmer waters than most alpine lakes. In winter, Nassfeld is one of Carinthia's largest ski resorts with 110 km of slopes.

The ascent to Gartnerkofel (2,195 m) by cable car then on foot opens onto an exceptional border panorama: on one side Carinthia and its lakes, on the other the Italian Friulian Dolomites. In clear weather, the view extends to the Adriatic.

activities.text: Hiking or mountain biking on Nassfeld massif's marked trails. Marked routes range from family-friendly valley-floor walks to demanding ridge hikes. The downhill mountain bike trails from Gartnerkofel are among Carinthia's most technical.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

visits.text: The scent of must floats all the way to the car park: welcome to Poysdorf, historic heart of the Weinviertel. The Vino Versum, housed in a baroque Bürgerspital, traces local viticulture from its medieval origins. The interactive exhibition on pressing techniques surprises more than you'd expect. Open April to October, reduced rate with the NÖ Card.

The earliest wine documentation from Poysdorf dates to the Middle Ages, but it's the Radyweg that catches the eye: this Kellergasse with its dense green cover and slightly overgrown brambles offers a striking contrast to Mailberg's Kellergasse Zipf, all whitewashed lime. Two atmospheres of the same heritage, ten kilometres apart. Walkable from the town centre.

activities.text: Book the Vino Versum's tractor tour for a guided loop through the town's Kellergassen - a local guide takes about twenty people on a historic trailer and explains the wine-press traditions. Departures on Sundays and public holidays at 4pm, duration 1h30, glass of wine included at the end. Advance booking recommended.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: The Kuenringer said: never taken, never. Burg Rappottenstein withstood three sieges without falling, since its construction in the 12th century on a granite rock in the valley of the Little Kamp. It has belonged since 1664 to the Counts of Abensperg und Traun, who have preserved its medieval character with its five outer courtyards and Italian-inspired arcaded galleries on three levels.

Three styles in one: Romanesque, Gothic, Renaissance. In the interior rooms of Burg Rappottenstein, secular frescoes from the 16th century are among the rarest examples of their kind in Austria - civil painting from this period has almost entirely disappeared. The Burgschenke in the courtyard serves regional cuisine after the visit.

activities.text: The Burgschenke prepares Waldviertler Schmankerl after the compulsory guided tour: roast veal with horseradish and Waldviertel Mohn Knödel with poppy seeds are the signature dishes. The guided tour included in the ticket (approx. 12 euros for adults) starts at fixed times in summer - allow 1h30 for the whole visit + refreshments.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: Retz has more cellars than streets: verifiable. Beneath the Hauptplatz, the network of wine cellars extends over tens of kilometres of galleries carved into the sand. The Retzer Erlebniskeller offers 1h30 guided tours at a constant 10-12°C - bring wool even in July. The Hauptplatzerklärung included in the ticket provides historical context before the descent. Closed Mondays.

The Retz windmill, at the northern edge of town, is the only one still operational in Austria for grinding grain. Built in the 18th century, it still turns during guided tours from April to October. The view from the promontory embraces the entire Retzer Land as far as Bohemia. Tour with wine tasting: 45 minutes, around 8 euros.

activities.text: Go down into the Retzer Erlebniskeller with the evening tour group: the dramatic lighting in the sandstone galleries beats the brochure photos hands down. On Fridays from April to October, the Nachtwächter also offers a nocturnal tour of the old town at 8pm from Hauptplatz 30. Duration 1h30, 14 euros, in German but easy to follow.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: Perched on a basalt cone over 2 million years old, Riegersburg was never taken by storm in several centuries of existence. 7 gates, 11 bastions, 4 km of walls - the numbers are enough to understand why. The climb on foot via the historic path from the village takes 20 minutes, but every step is worth it for the views over the Vulkanland.

Riegersburg Castle houses three museums: the Burgmuseum (17th-century state rooms), the Hexenmuseum (witchcraft trials) and the Waffenmuseum (arsenal from the Middle Ages to the Renaissance). This is also where the legend of 'die schlimme Liesl', the lady of the castle from 1637 to 1672 known for her dissolute ways, is told.

activities.text: Climb on foot via the historic path rather than the cable car - the view gradually opens over the volcanic hills and vineyards of eastern Styria. The birds of prey demonstration (Greifvogelwarte) takes place daily in fine weather: spectacular, and not tacky.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Styria - Ausseerland, Gesäuse and wine country

visits.text: The Rosenburg falconry: the kind of show that makes you feel like you were there five hundred years ago. Falconers in Renaissance costume release eagles and falcons twice daily in the tournament courtyard, at 11am and 3pm in season. Austrian falconry - of which Rosenburg is one of the emblematic sites - has been listed as UNESCO intangible heritage since 2012. Entrance ticket 17 euros for adults, includes guided tour of the castle or the show of your choice.

The tournament courtyard of Schloss Rosenburg is the largest still entirely preserved in Europe: built between 1593 and 1597, it still hosts tournaments and medieval spectacles. The castle itself is first mentioned in 1175, and its state apartments contain a little-known but impressive collection of weapons from the 15th to 18th centuries.

activities.text: Stay for the flight session: twice daily, April-October, in the tournament courtyard. Arrive 15 minutes early to position yourself correctly: the stands are small and the afternoon sun hits backlit for the 3pm session - prefer the 11am one for photos. The adjacent David Austin rose garden is freely accessible with your entrance ticket.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: On the chimneys, the clatter of storks: Rust houses Burgenland's largest stork colony, about 15 pairs returning every spring from Africa. The nests visible from the high street and the Conradplatz are the main attraction from March to August. The live Storchenkamera on the town's website allows you to follow the births.

The Fischerkirche of Rust is the town's oldest monument, with frescoes dating from the 12th century - it rises on the foundations of an old Roman observation post. The entire old town is listed as a historic monument, its 16th-19th century baroque merchant houses constitute an exceptional ensemble in the lake region.

activities.text: The Ausbruch of Rust: Burgenland's Sauternes, and some vintages dispute the title of best dessert wine in Central Europe. Botrytis develops thanks to the lake's humidity on the town's 450 hectares of vineyards. Several cellars open directly for tasting; the Weinakademie Österreich at the Seehof offers full-day seminars.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: Hohensalzburg Fortress, one of Europe's best-preserved fortresses, dominating the city from a rocky outcrop 120 meters high. Built in 1077 by Archbishop Gebhard, expanded through the 16th century. The princely apartments, armory, and panoramas over the Salzburg Alps from the ramparts justify the funicular or foot ascent.

Salzburg Old Town (Altstadt), a UNESCO-listed exceptional Baroque ensemble erected under the prince-archbishops of the 17th century. Getreidegasse with its wrought-iron signs, Residenzplatz with its Baroque fountain, and numerous art galleries and boutiques compose a unique urban scenography.

Salzburg Cathedral (Dom), a North Italian Baroque masterpiece with three naves completed in 1628. The dome frescoes, monumental organs, and facade bronzes make it one of the Alps' most beautiful religious buildings. Mozart was baptized here on January 28, 1756.

Mirabell Gardens, a 17th-century French garden designed by Fischer von Erlach, with Hohensalzburg Fortress as backdrop. The geometric parterres, mythological statues, central basin, and fountains offer Salzburg's most famous photographic composition.

Mozart's Birthplace (Mozarts Geburtshaus), Getreidegasse 9—the third-floor apartment where Wolfgang Amadeus was born on January 27, 1756. The child's harpsichord, miniature violin, and original manuscript scores are among the most touching pieces in this biographical museum.

Residenz of the Prince-Archbishops (Residenz), a Baroque palace of 180 rooms occupying an entire side of Residenzplatz. The Prince's Gallery houses a remarkable collection of Rembrandt, Rubens, and Flemish paintings. The grand reception hall with its illusionist frescoes is one of Austria's most impressive Baroque spaces.

Hellbrunn, a 17th-century pleasure palace 4 km from the center with its famous Wasserspiele (mechanical water games). Archbishop Markus Sittikus installed 265 hidden water jets to surprise his guests during banquets. Still in working order, these hydraulic automata are unique in Europe.

activities.text: Walk on the Mönchsberg, a wooded hill accessible by elevator from the old town (€3). Aerial views over the entire city and castle from the Café Winkler terrace. Museum of Modern Art (Museum der Moderne) at the summit with contemporary collection.

Mozart concert or recital in one of Salzburg's historic settings: Mozarteum, Residenz, or Mirabell Palace. Daily chamber concerts in period costume in 18th-century salons are an authentic, non-touristy experience.

Tour of the Salzburg lakes—excursion to the nearby Salzkammergut (St. Wolfgang, Mondsee, Wolfgangsee) 40-50 km from Salzburg. Mondsee with its Baroque church (setting for The Sound of Music) and St. Wolfgang with Pacher's altarpiece (1481) are accessible in a half-day.

Salzburg market and gastronomy at the Grünmarkt (daily market) near the cathedral. Specialties: authentic Fürst Mozartkugeln (not the aluminum foil version), peasant bread, alpine cheeses, and Stiegl Goldbräu on tap in the Mönchsberg beer gardens.
