places.name: Sankt Veit An Der Glan

visits.text: Hochosterwitz Castle ranks among Austria's most spectacular fortresses: a medieval stronghold perched atop a 160-meter limestone pinnacle dominating the Carinthian plain, visible for dozens of kilometers in every direction. Built from 860 AD and remodeled in the 16th century by the Khevenhüller family, it has remained virtually unchanged since. The 620-meter helical access road climbs through 14 successive fortified gates, each equipped with different defensive mechanisms—portcullises, machicolations, drawbridges—which, according to tradition, kept the castle impregnable for centuries.

The interior rooms open to visitors display collections of armor, paintings, and prehistoric artifacts. The castle chapel, built in 1571, preserves its original frescoes. An elevator from the parking area allows visitors with mobility limitations direct access to the main entrance.

activities.text: Swimming at Lake Längsee, a few kilometers from the castle. This small, clean, uncrowded lake offers a refreshing pause after exploring the fortress. Its wooded shores and clear water make it an ideal family stop.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

Sankt Veit an der Glan Hauptplatz

visits.text: The Krimml Falls (Krimmler Wasserfälle) plunge in three successive cascades of 140, 100, and 140 meters—380 meters total—from a hanging valley at 1,470 meters altitude. They're Europe's highest waterfalls and the world's fifth tallest. The 4-km hiking trail, well-maintained with viewing platforms, reaches the lower cascade in 15 minutes, the upper one in about an hour. The constant mist from water crashing on rock creates a unique atmosphere, and the vegetation of mosses and giant ferns lining the banks reinforces the feeling of entering another world.

Hohe Tauern National Park, with its 1,856 km² of alpine wilderness, shelters the Grossglockner (3,798 m), Austria's highest peak, glaciers, ibex, marmots, and golden eagles. The Mittersill visitor center features interactive exhibits on protected flora and fauna. Rangers organize guided outings to discover the alpine ecosystem.

Lake Wörthersee, a stone's throw from Villach, is one of Europe's warmest alpine lakes—its waters reach 28°C in summer thanks to sub-lacustrine thermal springs. Its shores are equipped with promenades and beaches, and the villages of Pörtschach and Velden offer a Belle Époque setting with their century-old villas and hotels.

activities.text: Family swimming and paddleboarding on Lake Wörthersee. Rental operators offer pedal boats, kayaks, and stand-up paddleboards on the beaches at Pörtschach and Maria Wörth. For children, supervised swimming areas with shallow bottoms are safe.

⚠️ Logistics note: Krimml Falls are 120 km from Villach. Day 3 is very packed: it's recommended to leave early for the falls, pack a picnic on-site, and reach Villach and its lakes late afternoon. Consider spending the night in the Salzburgerland (Zell am See or Mittersill) for more balanced logistics.

days_plan.itin_title: Family Road Trip Through Austria: Mountains, Lakes & Imperial Palaces
Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

visits.text: Hans Hollein's glass cube perched on the tower of Schloss Rothschild in 2007 is an architectural statement that still divides Waidhofen residents. From the cube, the view embraces the medieval old town, the Ybbs and the hills of the Mostviertel in a dizzying panorama. The Schloss also houses the museum of the 5 elements - Feuer, Wasser, Erde, Holz, Metall - legacy of the 2007 Landesausstellung.

Town of blacksmiths since the Middle Ages, Waidhofen an der Ybbs was a hub of the Eisenstraße - the alpine iron road. The forge workshops are still active and some craftsmen receive visitors. The old town called 'Stadt der sieben Türme' preserves its ramparts, four bell towers and three civic towers almost intact.

activities.text: Stroll through the old town with the Niederösterreich-Card in hand: it gives access to Schloss Rothschild, the 5-Elemente-Museum and covers several regional attractions. The Wochenmarkt is held three times a week in the main square - vegetables, cheeses and Mostbirnensaft, the local pear cider. Take an hour to wander the alleys before heading back towards Lunz.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: Weissensee is Carinthia's highest (930 m) and most pristine natural lake, in the Carnic Alps. Stretching 11.6 km long, only one-third of its shores are accessible—the rest remains completely wild and protected. Water quality is exceptional, with underwater visibility reaching 10 meters in summer, making it one of Europe's clearest lakes.

In winter, Weissensee transforms into one of Europe's largest natural skating rinks with 6.2 km² of frozen surface. The natural ice reaches 40 to 50 cm thick and hosts the annual Weissensee Skating Marathon, one of Europe's oldest and most popular.

activities.text: Swimming, kayaking, and hiking on the wilder, quieter eastern shore. The western shore, around Techendorf village, gathers hotels and developed beaches. The lake circuit on foot or by bike is a half-day excursion revealing the contrasts between both shores.
Hike to Granattor from Techendorf—marked 3-hour trail offering plunging views of the entire lake from the Carnic Alps ridges.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

visits.text: In 1321, King Friedrich der Schöne granted Weitra the exclusive right to brew beer within a radius of one mile - making the town Austria's oldest Braustadt. This privilege, still displayed in the Schlossmuseum in the Renaissance castle, lies at the origin of an uninterrupted brewing tradition. The Bierkirtag festival is held every summer in the main square.

In the Renaissance courtyard of Schloss Weitra, the smell of hops from the beer museum drifts down from the vats displayed upstairs. The castle, with medieval origins, took its Renaissance form under the Kuenringer before passing to the Fürstenberger. The lion-headed arcades on three levels bear witness. Climbing the bell tower offers a view over the medieval old town and its ramparts.

activities.text: Take the Biertour with Weitra's night watchman: the local Nachtwächter accompanies visitors along the historic Biermeile several times a week from May to October. The route passes through former taverns and underground beer cellars. Info and booking via weitra-tourismus.at - programme available from spring.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: Schönbrunn Palace, nicknamed the "Viennese Versailles," was the Habsburg summer residence for centuries. Its 1,441 rooms aren't all open to visitors, but Franz Joseph and Elisabeth's (Sissi's) apartments, Maria Theresa's state rooms, and rococo chinoiserie décor constitute a complete immersion into imperial court life. The Children's Museum lets little ones dress as princes and princesses and learn the games and customs of imperial children.

Schönbrunn Gardens, open free from 6:30 a.m., stretch 1.2 km from east to west. The Gloriette, a triumphal colonnade atop the hill, offers a panoramic view over Vienna. The topiary maze and classified fountains are perfect for a family stroll.

The Sisi Museum (Hofburg) plunges into Empress Elisabeth's intimate life through her original dresses, letters, and portraits. The Sisi Museum, located in the Hofburg, reveals the complex and often melancholic personality behind the legend: her strict diets, incessant travels, and difficult relationship with court etiquette.

The Haus der Musik (House of Music) is an interactive museum housed in a downtown palace. Each floor is dedicated to a Viennese composer—Haydn, Mozart, Beethoven, Schubert, Strauss, Mahler. Children can compose their own waltz, conduct the virtual Vienna Philharmonic Orchestra, or hear music through their own heartbeat.

St. Stephen's Cathedral (Stephansdom), built from 1160 in regional limestone, is Vienna's symbol. Its multicolored tile roof forms spectacular geometric patterns visible from the Old Town rooftops. The catacombs beneath the cathedral hold the bones of 11,000 Viennese victims of 17th-century epidemics.

activities.text: Picnic and stroll in the Prater, Vienna's vast popular park on the Danube banks. The Wurstelprater, an amusement park opened in 1766, houses the Giant Ferris Wheel (Riesenrad) built in 1897, rising 65 meters: a panoramic view over Vienna from its steel cabins. The 250 attractions—carousels, haunted houses, roller coasters—delight families.

Schönbrunn Zoo (Tiergarten), founded in 1752, is the world's oldest zoo still in operation. Over 700 animal species, including giant pandas, African elephants, and white rhinos, occupy enclosures redesigned as natural habitats. Daily programs let children observe animals up close during feeding times.

places.name: Wolayersee

visits.text: Wolayersee is one of Carinthia's most beautiful mountain lakes, perched at altitude on the Austrian-Italian border in the Carnic Alps. The lake is accessible after a 3-hour hike from Hubertuskapelle chapel (trailhead with parking). The crystal-clear blue-green water, alpine meadows, and surrounding peaks compose a tableau of striking beauty. A lakeside refuge offers food and accommodation.
Hubertuskapelle chapel, at the trail's start, merits a stop before beginning the ascent. This small 18th-century alpine chapel is a traditional pilgrimage site for regional hunters and shepherds.

activities.text: Round-trip hike Hubertuskapelle - Wolayersee: 3 hours up, 2.5 hours down, moderate elevation gain. Arrive early for parking (limited spaces). Picnic or meal at the lake refuge. Bring hiking boots and warm clothing—the lake sits above 1,800 m.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

map_keywords: Wolayersee Alpine lake border Italy Carinthia

visits.text: Wörthersee, 19.4 km long, is Carinthia's most famous lake and one of Austria's most exclusive bathing destinations. Its waters reach 28°C in summer thanks to its sheltered location. Since the Belle Époque, the Austro-Hungarian aristocracy built sumptuous Art Nouveau villas along its shores—Brahms composed his Second Symphony here in Pörtschach in 1877.

Pyramidenkogel, inaugurated in 2013, is the world's tallest wooden observation tower at 100 meters high. An elevator reaches the panoramic platform in 30 seconds: 360° views of the entire Wörthersee, neighboring lakes, and the Carinthian Alps. The descent via the 120-meter spiral slide is an attraction in itself.

Velden am Wörthersee, nicknamed the "Austrian Monte Carlo," boasts its historic casino and the Grand Hotel Schloss Velden (1590, converted into a luxury hotel). Its elegant lakeside promenade and terraces animate Carinthian evenings.

activities.text: Swimming at the beaches of Pörtschach or Maria Wörth. The Maria Wörth peninsula, with its 10th-century church reflected in the lake, is the iconic image of Wörthersee. Public beaches are free to access.

days_plan.itin_title: Carinthia Austria Road Trip: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Passes & Medieval Castles

places.name: Zell Am See

visits.text: Lake Zell (Zeller See), a 4.5 km by 1.5 km expanse of water cradled between the Kitzsteinhorn massif (3,203 m) and the Schmittenhöhe (1,965 m). The complete lakeside promenade covers 11.3 km and takes 2.5 hours on a flat, level trail. The turquoise waters reach 24°C in July-August.

Town of Zell am See, a medieval borough founded in the 8th century by monks. The Romanesque tower of St. Hippolytus Church (12th century) dominates the old town. The lanes of the historic center with their shops and restaurants contrast with the alpine panoramas all around.

Schmittenhöhe cable car (1,965 m), 15-minute ascent from the town center. At the summit, the Pinzgauer Spaziergang panoramic trail offers views of 30 peaks over 3,000 meters including the Grossglockner. Mountain restaurant with sunny terrace.

activities.text: Grossglockner High Alpine Road (48 km, toll ~€36), a scenic route with 36 numbered hairpin turns crossing Hohe Tauern National Park. Start from Fusch an der Glocknerstraße. Must-stop viewpoints: Edelweißspitze (2,577 m, 360° panorama) and Kaiser-Franz-Josefs-Höhe (2,369 m) for views of the Pasterze Glacier.

Swimming in Lake Zell from the public beach at Seespitz or the municipal campground beach. Pedal boats, sailboats, and paddleboards for rent on the west shore. Water temperature 22-24°C in July-August.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 days in Austria: Wachau Valley, Alpine Peaks & Salzburg

map_keywords: Pfarrkirche St. Hippolyt Zell am See

visits.text: The Zillertal valley opens east of Innsbruck, framed by peaks exceeding 3,500 meters. Its villages—Zell am Ziller, Mayrhofen, Finkenberg—preserve their large white-façade houses and pointed bell towers. In Zell am Ziller, don't miss the Erlebnis-Sennerei Zillertal cheese dairy: an interactive exhibit explains how alpine cheeses have been made for centuries.

Alpbach, a neighboring village in the Alpbachtal valley, has been voted Austria's most beautiful village several times. A local law forbids any building material other than wood: the result is rare architectural harmony. Geranium-covered balconies, stone fountains, and barns attached to houses compose a postcard scene that the years have not dimmed.

Rattenberg, at the valley entrance, is Austria's smallest town with only 400 inhabitants. Renowned since the Middle Ages for glass craftsmanship, its medieval lanes and baroque Augustinian church merit a stop. Artisan blown-glass workshops and boutiques line the main street.

activities.text: Family hike in the Alpbachtal on marked trails departing from Alpbach village. To gain altitude quickly without effort, the Wiedersbergerhornbahn gondolas take families up to panoramas over the Zillertal Alps and Lake Achensee. From the upper station, an hour's walk reaches the 2,127-meter summit.

Board the Zillertalbahn, a historic steam train linking Jenbach to Mayrhofen over 32 km, crossing 35 bridges above the Ziller River. At a maximum 35 km/h, the wooden carriages open onto meadows and valley villages. Children love this journey in a vintage locomotive.

days_plan.itin_title: Family Road Trip Through Austria: Mountains, Lakes & Imperial Palaces

visits.text: The cloister loop along the Kamp: absolute silence, paired columns and hexagonal fountain house dating from the 13th century. At Stift Zwettl, founded in 1138 by Hadmar von Kuenring, this Romanesque-Gothic passage is the only one of its kind still intact outside southern France. The 12th-century chapter house - vaulted by a single granite column - is the oldest in Austria. Open from late March to late October.

Stift Zwettl is one of the oldest Cistercian abbeys in the world never to have interrupted its monastic activity since its foundation. The baroque library built in 1730-1732 by Joseph Munggenast, with its some 28,000 volumes and Paul Troger frescoes, is accessible only on guided tours. The treasury room displays the French 13th-century ivory Madonna.

activities.text: Buy smoked carp at the abbey shop: the fish ponds have been continuously farmed since the Middle Ages, and the shop sells their fresh or smoked fish on Saturday mornings from 9am to noon. Perfect for a picnic in the abbot's garden. The Internationale Konzerttage take place every July - programme on stift-zwettl.at.

days_plan.itin_title: Austria's Frontier Lands - Waldviertel & Burgenland

visits.text: Edelweiss-Spitze (2,571 m) is the highest viewpoint on the road - a short detour off the main route that most people skip. The 360° panorama from the stone tower covers over 30 peaks above 3,000 m. On a clear morning, you can see into three countries. No entrance fee beyond the road toll.

days_plan.itin_title: Luxury Alpine Road Trip: Zurich to Salzburg — 14 Days

visits.text: The Grossglockner High Alpine Road is Austria's answer to the Stelvio - 48 km, 36 hairpins, climbing to 2,504 m at the Hochtor tunnel. But where the Stelvio is aggressive, the Grossglockner is majestic: wider, better surfaced, and with built-in viewpoints every few kilometres. Budget 4 hours minimum - not because the road demands it, but because the stops will.

activities.text: Drive the northern descent toward Ferleiten - gentler bends, greener pastures, and alpine meadows thick with wildflowers in June-July. The toll is €46.50 per car (2026 prices). If you want to drive back up and down again, your ticket is valid for the full calendar day.

days_plan.itin_title: Luxury Alpine Road Trip: Zurich to Salzburg — 14 Days

visits.text: A slender Gothic church spire against a wall of snow-capped peaks - that's Heiligenblut, the southern gateway to the Grossglockner High Alpine Road and one of Austria's most photographed villages. The Pfarrkirche (Church of St. Vincent) dates from the 15th century and houses a remarkable winged altarpiece by the workshop of Michael Pacher. Five minutes to see, hard to forget.

activities.text: 🍽️ Quick stop for coffee and strudel at Café Dorfstub'm in Heiligenblut's centre before tackling the Grossglockner road. Homemade apple strudel with vanilla sauce - proper Austrian fuel.

🏨 NIGHT - Chalet Hotel Senger. Family-run 4-star at the foot of the Grossglockner. Wood-panelled rooms with mountain views, excellent half-board restaurant using local game and herbs. The terrace faces the Pfarrkirche spire.

days_plan.itin_title: Luxury Alpine Road Trip: Zurich to Salzburg — 14 Days

visits.text: The air at the summit of the Hintertuxer Gletscher is thin and dry even in July: you're over 3,000 meters and skiers in shorts rub shoulders with mountaineers in down jackets. The glacier is visibly receding from one season to the next. Visit it before the picture changes again.

The Hintertux glacier is one of the few Alpine glaciers skiable year-round, accessible from Hintertux by cable car in several sections. National ski teams practice their off-season training here. Check conditions on hintertuxer-gletscher.at.

activities.text: If you come in summer, the upper glacier slopes are virtually deserted outside training groups. The climb to the Tuxer Fernerhaus at 3,250 meters takes 45 minutes. Dress warm: even in August, the summit temperature hovers around 0°C.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Alpine Traverse, 16 days: Grisons to Trieste

visits.text: Kaiser-Franz-Josefs-Höhe is the grand finale: a terrace facing Austria's highest peak (Grossglockner, 3,798 m) and the Pasterze Glacier, the largest glacier in the Eastern Alps. The glacier has retreated dramatically - comparison photos from the 1900s at the visitor centre are sobering. A short trail leads closer to the ice, but watch for loose rock. The marmots here are completely fearless.

activities.text: 🍽️ LUNCH - Panoramarestaurant Kaiser-Franz-Josefs-Höhe. Not fine dining by any stretch, but the terrace has arguably the most dramatic lunch view in Austria. Stick to simple dishes: Kaiserschmarren (shredded pancake), Wiener Schnitzel, goulash soup. The Grossglockner right in front of you does the rest.

days_plan.itin_title: Luxury Alpine Road Trip: Zurich to Salzburg — 14 Days

visits.text: If you see only one thing in Salzburg, make it the Hohensalzburg Fortress - it dominates the city from a cliff 120 m above the Old Town. The largest fully preserved castle in Central Europe, dating from 1077. Take the Festungsbahn funicular up, explore the state rooms, the medieval torture chamber (grimly fascinating), and the ramparts with views across the Salzach to the Bavarian Alps. Allow 2 hours; the audioguide is worth it.

Mozart's Birthplace (Geburtshaus) at Getreidegasse 9 is where Wolfgang Amadeus was born in 1756 and lived until he was 17. The museum is small - three floors of original instruments, letters and family portraits. The violin he played as a child is behind glass on the second floor. Skip the souvenir shop, which is overpriced even by Salzburg standards.

Salzburg Cathedral (Salzburger Dom) is a 17th-century Baroque beast - the dome is 71 m high and the interior swallows crowds whole. Mozart was baptised in the font near the entrance in 1756. The organ loft houses five organs that play together during the noon concerts. If you time it right, attend one - tickets are under €10.

Mirabell Palace and Gardens were built in 1606 as a love token from an archbishop to his mistress. The gardens are pure geometric Baroque - hedges, fountains, dwarf statues and a clear sightline to the fortress on the hill opposite. Free entry. Film buffs: this is where 'Do-Re-Mi' was filmed in The Sound of Music.

Hellbrunn Palace (Schloss Hellbrunn) is 20 minutes south of the centre and completely different from Mirabell's Baroque formality. Built in 1615 as a pleasure palace, it's famous for the Wasserspiele - trick fountains designed to soak unsuspecting guests at the dinner table. 400 years later, they still work and they still get you wet. The park is free; the fountain tour costs about €15 and is genuinely entertaining.

Hangar-7 is worth visiting even if you dined at Ikarus last night. The Red Bull-owned glass dome houses a rotating collection of historic aircraft, Formula 1 cars (including Sebastian Vettel's), and Felix Baumgartner's Stratos space-jump capsule. Free entry. The architecture alone - a futuristic steel-and-glass shell near the airport runway - is worth the drive.

activities.text: Walk through the Getreidegasse - Salzburg's narrowest and most atmospheric shopping street, hung with ornate wrought-iron guild signs dating back centuries. Every luxury brand has an outpost here, but the real finds are the small independent shops in the connecting passageways (Durchhäuser). The Stiftsbäckerei at St. Peter's has been baking bread since 1160 - oldest bakery in Austria.

🍽️ LUNCH - Stiftskeller St. Peter. Austria's oldest restaurant - documented since 803 AD (yes, the year 803). Charlemagne allegedly dined here. The vaulted rooms feel like a medieval banquet hall. Order the Tafelspitz (boiled beef in broth) or the Salzburger Nockerl for dessert - a soufflé the size of a football. Touristy? Slightly. Worth it? Absolutely.

🍽️ DINNER - Restaurant Ikarus at Hangar-7. Two Michelin stars, and unlike any restaurant you've visited: a glass-domed aircraft hangar housing Red Bull's collection of vintage planes, helicopters and Formula 1 cars. Every month a different world-class guest chef takes over the kitchen - you literally don't know whose food you'll be eating until you arrive. Only 8 tables since the 2025 refit. Wealthy foodies fly in from across Europe for this.

🏨 NIGHT - Hotel Sacher Salzburg. On the banks of the Salzach with direct views of the Old Town and fortress. Same crystal chandeliers and brocade as Vienna's legendary Sacher. Sachertorte waiting in your room at turndown.

🍽️ LUNCH - Gasthof Goldgasse. Hidden in the Old Town on a tiny alley, this restaurant features in the Michelin Guide and serves modern Austrian cuisine with real personality. Almost exclusively Austrian-sourced ingredients. The lunch menu is shorter and more affordable than dinner - a smart move on your last day.

Last walk along the Salzach riverbank toward Makartsteg (the padlock bridge). The view back toward the Old Town with the fortress lit up is Salzburg's postcard shot. If it's your last evening, the Sacher Bar's terrace has the same view with a cocktail in hand.

🍽️ DINNER - Esszimmer. One Michelin star, family-run, and the heart of Salzburg's local fine dining scene. Chef Andreas Kaiblinger describes it as 'honouring grandmother's recipes in a modern language.' The tasting menu changes seasonally; expect game, trout, Alpine herbs, and Austrian wines you won't find outside the country. Intimate, warm, no pretension.

🏨 NIGHT - Hotel Sacher Salzburg (second night). Before checkout tomorrow, breakfast in the winter garden - white tablecloths, silver service, best Viennoiserie in the city. Salzburg Airport is 10 minutes by taxi.

days_plan.itin_title: Luxury Alpine Road Trip: Zurich to Salzburg — 14 Days
Luxury Alpine Road Trip: Zurich to Salzburg — 14 Days

visits.text: Up to 6,659 spectators per night, a floating stage that changes with each production: Bregenz's Seebühne is the world's largest lake stage. In 2026, it's Verdi's La Traviata, premiere on July 22. Even outside festival season, the stage can be viewed from the promenade.

Take the Pfänder cable car to climb to 1,064 meters: the view plunges over the entire lake, from Konstanz to Lindau, with the Alps as backdrop. A panoramic trail circles the summit in 30 minutes. Allow €14 round-trip.

If you climb behind the lakefront, Bregenz's medieval Oberstadt keeps its ramparts, its Saint Martin's tower, and its cobbled lanes. Few tourists venture here. The Kunsthaus Bregenz (KUB), Peter Zumthor's glass cube, is just below.

activities.text: At sunset, the empty tiers of the Seebühne and the lake take on an orange tint. Even without an opera ticket, the promenade along the Festspielhaus offers the spectacle. Bars and restaurants on the Seepromenade.

Bregenz locals call the Pfänder their "Hausberg." At the summit, a small alpine zoo (free entry) presents ibex, marmots, and deer. The panoramic restaurant serves Käsespätzle with views across four countries.
